chunk_summary: Chronic diarrhea in Indian practice requires a two-stage evaluation: stool characterization and targeted etiologic testing, alongside nutritional support and selective treatment based on specific triggers.
learning_objectives: Understand the definition and stages of chronic diarrhea evaluation in Indian practice.
Learn nutritional strategies to prevent secondary malnutrition during chronic diarrhea.
Identify key diagnostic tests for stool characterization and common etiologies in India.
Recognize referral triggers for systemic or immunodeficiency-associated diarrhea.
key_facts: Chronic diarrhea is defined as lasting more than 14 days.
ORS, zinc supplementation, and energy-dense feeding are essential during treatment.
Avoid routine antibiotics unless dysentery or systemic bacterial illness is suspected.
Stool pH and reducing substances can assess watery diarrhea in resource-limited settings.
Screen for celiac disease, parasitic infections, and TB in chronic diarrhea cases.
HIV testing is recommended for immunodeficiency-associated diarrhea with recurrent infections.
reference_quote: Persistent or chronic diarrhea should be evaluated in two stages: characterize the diarrhea type, then pursue targeted etiologic testing. Start treatment while evaluating: continue age-appropriate feeding, use ORS during episodes, give zinc, and increase energy density to prevent secondary malnutrition. (Indian Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Referral triggers for systemic or immunodeficiency-associated diarrhea
